topic: Urban Planning in the Amazonian Metropolis
title: The Methods Behind Uncovering a Lost City in the Amazon Rainforest with LiDAR Scanning and Archaeological Excavation

lesson.learn: Between 2019 and 2020, an international team of archaeologists led by researchers including Dr. José Iriarte (University of Exeter) and supported by institutions such as the British Museum used LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) technology alongside targeted archaeological excavation to reveal an extensive pre-Columbian urban settlement hidden beneath the dense Amazon rainforest canopy. The LiDAR surveys mapped terrain beneath vegetation cover, exposing a complex network of raised roadways, geometric earthworks, agricultural terraces, and ceremonial platforms. The site, estimated to have been occupied between approximately 200 BCE and 1500 CE, spans over 1.2 square kilometers and contains evidence of more than 200 structural features including plazas, residential compounds, and possible ritual centers. The discovery demonstrates that the Amazon was not an untouched wilderness but a landscape shaped by large, organized societies with advanced environmental engineering and urban planning systems.
lesson.simpler: Scientists used airborne laser scanning (LiDAR) and excavation to find a hidden ancient city in the Amazon rainforest. The technology allowed them to see shapes under the trees that were impossible to spot from the ground. They discovered roads, raised platforms, homes, and farming areas, showing that thousands of years ago, people built and lived in organized cities deep in the jungle. These people also created systems to manage water and grow food in a difficult rainforest environment, proving they were highly skilled at adapting to nature.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The discovery of the Amazonian urban settlement challenges the long-standing assumption that the Amazon rainforest was historically sparsely populated and environmentally untouched. Instead, it reveals that complex societies engineered large-scale landscapes using sustainable agricultural systems, road networks, and water management strategies. The integration of LiDAR technology with traditional archaeology demonstrates how modern scientific tools can transform understanding of ancient civilizations. This finding highlights the importance of interdisciplinary research in reconstructing lost histories and shows that urban planning and environmental adaptation were central to pre-Columbian societies in South America.
lesson.deeperDive: LiDAR technology works by emitting laser pulses from aircraft to measure ground elevation beneath forest canopy, allowing researchers to identify hidden human-made structures without clearing vegetation. In the Amazon basin, this method has revealed extensive networks of geoglyphs, raised causeways, canals, and settlement clusters previously obscured by dense rainforest. At the identified site, spatial analysis indicates deliberate urban planning, including aligned structures, central plazas, and distributed residential zones connected by engineered roadways. Evidence of raised fields and drainage systems suggests sophisticated agricultural engineering designed to manage seasonal flooding and nutrient-poor soils. Comparative analysis with other pre-Columbian sites such as the Llanos de Mojos in Bolivia and Geoglyph sites in Acre, Brazil, suggests that Amazonian societies developed regionally diverse but technologically sophisticated landscape management strategies. These findings align with broader archaeological evidence indicating that pre-Columbian populations significantly modified large portions of the Amazon through controlled burning, soil enhancement (terra preta), and engineered water systems. The discovery also supports the idea that the Amazon rainforest is partly a cultural landscape shaped by long-term human occupation rather than a purely natural ecosystem.
lesson.funFact: Some Amazonian archaeological sites are so large that they can only be fully mapped using aircraft-based LiDAR, because the dense rainforest canopy makes ground visibility almost impossible even from a few meters away.

people: José Iriarte, archaeologist specializing in pre-Columbian Amazonian societies and landscape modification
Stephen Rostain, archaeologist known for research on ancient Amazonian geoglyphs and settlement systems
Mark Robinson, environmental archaeologist contributing to studies of Amazonian land use and settlement patterns
William Balée, anthropologist studying long-term human impact on Amazonian ecosystems
British Museum research teams, interdisciplinary contributors to archaeological interpretation and artifact preservation
places: Amazon Rainforest, South America, a vast biome containing evidence of extensive pre-Columbian human modification
Amazon Basin, Brazil and surrounding countries, region where LiDAR has revealed hidden ancient settlement networks
Acre, Brazil, site of numerous geoglyphs and earthworks linked to ancient Amazonian civilizations
Llanos de Mojos, Bolivia, a floodplain region with raised agricultural fields and engineered landscape systems
British Museum, London, UK, institution involved in global archaeological research and artifact analysis
years: 200 BCE: Approximate early development period of some Amazonian settlement systems
200–1500 CE: Main occupation period of many identified Amazonian urban and agricultural landscapes
2010s CE: Expansion of LiDAR use in archaeological surveying of rainforest environments
2019–2020 CE: Major LiDAR-based discoveries and mapping projects revealing hidden Amazonian settlements
21st century CE: Ongoing research reshaping understanding of pre-Columbian Amazonian civilizations
concepts: LiDAR remote sensing, airborne laser scanning technology used to map terrain beneath dense vegetation
Landscape archaeology, the study of how ancient societies shaped and interacted with their environments
Pre-Columbian urbanism, the development of complex cities and settlements in the Americas before European contact
Anthropogenic landscape modification, long-term human alteration of natural ecosystems through agriculture and construction
Sustainable ancient engineering, historical methods of adapting infrastructure to environmental constraints
Geoglyph construction, large-scale earthworks created for ceremonial, territorial, or astronomical purposes
Amazonian terraforming practices, soil and water management techniques used to enhance agricultural productivity
